questions.specification:
Motion $X$ shows circumduction at the shoulder joint.

Which biomechanical principle best explains the functional advantage of this movement?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/MYmXRMSfQZVBO9CZi76a_)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.1 Anatomical position, planes and movement  > B.1.1.2 Planes of movement and axes of rotation in human motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.1.2 Planes of movement and axes of rotation in human motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The diagram and options point to multi-directional control of movement (e.g., throwing/serving/swimming), which is explained biomechanically by movement occurring in multiple planes with associated axes of rotation (e.g., circumduction/ball-and-socket joint allowing motion across planes).

options.content: It limits movement to rotation about the longitudinal axis only, improving precision in turning actions
options.markscheme: Circumduction is a combined movement (flexion, extension, abduction and adduction) resulting in a circular path of the distal limb; it is not rotation about a single axis only.

options.content: It allows force generation along a single fixed axis for maximal stability in compound lifts
options.markscheme: Circumduction involves movement across multiple planes/axes rather than force generation along one fixed axis.

options.content: It increases movement efficiency by reducing joint range of motion across multiple planes
options.markscheme: Circumduction increases the available range of motion and the directions a limb can move, rather than reducing range of motion.

options.content: It enables a wide range of directional control, crucial for skills like throwing, serving, and swimming
options.markscheme: Correct. Circumduction allows the distal end of a limb to move in a circular path, providing versatility and directional control in multi-planar movements.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.2 Structure and function of connective tissues and joints > B.1.2.1 Connective tissues and joint structures supporting movement
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.2.1 Connective tissues and joint structures supporting movement
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts b–d focus on synovial joint movements, anatomical terms for body position, and the roles of tendons and ligaments—core content on joint/connective tissue structure and function supporting movement.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.3 Muscular function > B.1.3.2 Sliding filament theory and molecular basis of muscle contraction (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.3.2 Sliding filament theory and molecular basis of muscle contraction (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part e asks how skeletal muscle structure enables force production, aligning with muscle ultrastructure and the sliding filament/molecular basis of contraction.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.4 Levers in movement and sport > B.1.4.1 Classes of levers and their applications in human movement and sport
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.4.1 Classes of levers and their applications in human movement and sport
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part f is explicitly about third-class levers and their function/application in sport, which fits classes of levers and applications in human movement.

parts.content: Identify three types of movement possible at synovial joints.
parts.markscheme:
* Flexion <marks>A1</marks>
* Extension <marks>A1</marks>
* Rotation <marks>A1</marks>

<note> (Other acceptable answers: Abduction, Adduction, Circumduction — any three) </note>

[3 marks total]

parts.content: State three anatomical terms used to describe body position.
parts.markscheme:
* Superior <marks>A1</marks>
* Inferior <marks>A1</marks>
* Anterior <marks>A1</marks>

<note> (Other acceptable answers: Posterior, Medial, Lateral, Proximal, Distal — any three) </note>

[3 marks total]

parts.content: Describe the role of tendons and ligaments in generating and controlling movement.
parts.markscheme:
* Tendons connect muscle to bone <marks>A1</marks>
* Transmit force from muscle contraction to bone <marks>A1</marks>
* Ligaments connect bone to bone <marks>A1</marks>
* Stabilize joints during movement <marks>A1</marks>

[4 marks total]

parts.content: Explain how the structure of skeletal muscle enables force production.
parts.markscheme:
* Skeletal muscle is striated and multinucleated <marks>A1</marks>
* Contains myofibrils with actin and myosin filaments <marks>A1</marks>
* Contracts via sliding filament mechanism <marks>A1</marks>
* Neuromuscular junction triggers action potential <marks>A1</marks>
* Produces force to move bones <marks>A1</marks>

[5 marks total]

parts.content: Describe the function and application of third-class levers in sport.
parts.markscheme:
* Effort is between fulcrum and load <marks>A1</marks>
* Allows large range of motion and speed <marks>A1</marks>
* Requires more force than the load <marks>A1</marks>
* Example: Biceps curl — elbow is fulcrum, weight is load, biceps apply effort <marks>A1</marks>
* Common in sporting movements like kicking, throwing, running <marks>A1</marks>

<note> Any sport as an example of application is acceptable </note>

[5 marks total]

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.2.1 Connective tissues and joint structures supporting movement

parts.content: Identify two frontal plane movements.
parts.markscheme:
- Abduction <marks>A1</marks>
- Adduction <marks>A1</marks>
- Lateral flexion <marks>A1</marks>
- Inversion or eversion <marks>A1</marks>
- Elevation or depression of the scapula <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for any of the above identified movements, up to a maximum of [2].</note>

parts.content: State two functions of synovial fluid.
parts.markscheme:
- **Lubrication**: reduces friction between the articular surfaces of the joint <marks>A1</marks>
- **Nutrient supply**: provides oxygen and nutrients to the articular cartilage <marks>A1</marks>
- **Waste removal**: removes metabolic by-products from the joint capsule <marks>A1</marks>
- **Shock absorption**: protects the joint by distributing pressure during movement <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for any of the above stated functions, up to a maximum of [2].</note>

parts.content: Describe how the sagittal plane relates to running mechanics.
parts.markscheme:
- The sagittal plane divides the body into left and right halves, and most running mechanics occur within this plane <marks>A1</marks>
- Movement involves forward and backward motion, such as the stride progression <marks>A1</marks>
- Key joint actions include flexion and extension at the hip, knee, and ankle (dorsiflexion/plantarflexion) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Explain the relationship between ligaments and injury prevention.
parts.markscheme:
- Ligaments connect bone to bone across a joint, providing passive stability <marks>A1</marks>
- They limit excessive or unwanted ranges of motion, such as preventing hyper-extension <marks>A1</marks>
- They help maintain correct joint alignment during dynamic activities <marks>A1</marks>
- By restricting joint movement to safe planes, they reduce the risk of dislocations and sprains <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Outline the differences between fast-twitch and slow-twitch muscle fibers.
parts.markscheme:
- Fast-twitch (Type II) fibers produce high force/power, whereas slow-twitch (Type I) produce lower force <marks>A1</marks>
- Fast-twitch fibers fatigue quickly, while slow-twitch fibers are highly fatigue-resistant <marks>A1</marks>
- Fast-twitch fibers have low mitochondrial density/aerobic capacity, whereas slow-twitch have high density/capacity <marks>A1</marks>
- Fast-twitch are suited for explosive anaerobic activities like sprinting, while slow-twitch are suited for prolonged aerobic activities like marathons <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Explain how understanding anatomical movements improves sports technique.
parts.markscheme:
- Coaches can identify and correct poor joint alignment to optimize power and force production <marks>A1</marks>
- Ensuring movement stays within correct planes of motion prevents inefficient or dangerous mechanics <marks>A1</marks>
- Knowledge of movements enables more effective muscle engagement for specific sporting tasks <marks>A1</marks>
- Analysis of movement improves overall biomechanical efficiency, leading to enhanced performance <marks>A1</marks>
- Understanding movements assists in developing targeted injury prevention and rehabilitation programs <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

options.markscheme: C

options.markscheme: C

questions.specification: Which anthropometric measure is most relevant when assessing risk of obesity?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.1 Anatomical position, planes and movement  > B.1.1.3 Anthropometry and its applications in sport and health science (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.1.3 Anthropometry and its applications in sport and health science (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which anthropometric measure best assesses obesity risk (waist-to-hip ratio), which falls under anthropometry and its applications in sport and health science.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. BMI is commonly used to classify overweight/obesity but is not the most relevant measure for obesity-related health risk compared with central adiposity measures.
Incorrect. Waist circumference is a key measure of central obesity and health risk, but this question targets the ratio-based measure often used to indicate fat distribution pattern.
Correct. Waist-to-hip ratio reflects body fat distribution (central/android adiposity), which is strongly associated with obesity-related health risks.
Incorrect. Waist-to-height ratio is also used to assess central adiposity risk, but it is not the keyed answer for this item.

questions.specification:
Which of the following correctly identifies the labeled arm movements A and B at the shoulder and the anatomical plane in which they occur?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/LYVfgjIf2VjkxvVXUPfy9.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.1 Anatomical position, planes and movement  > B.1.1.2 Planes of movement and axes of rotation in human motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.1.2 Planes of movement and axes of rotation in human motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to identify specific arm movements (e.g., flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, horizontal flexion/extension, pronation/supination) and the anatomical plane in which they occur, which is directly about planes of movement and axes of rotation.

options.content: A = Supination, B = Pronation; Transverse plane
options.markscheme: Supination/pronation are movements at the radioulnar joints in the transverse plane, not the labeled shoulder movements A and B.

options.content: A = Flexion, B = Extension; Sagittal plane
options.markscheme: Flexion/extension occur in the sagittal plane, but the labeled movements A and B show the arm moving away from and back toward the body (abduction/adduction).

options.content: A = Abduction, B = Adduction; Frontal plane
options.markscheme: A shows shoulder abduction (arm moving laterally away from the midline) and B shows shoulder adduction (returning toward the midline). These occur in the frontal (coronal) plane.

options.content: A = Horizontal extension, B = Horizontal flexion; Transverse plane
options.markscheme: Horizontal flexion/extension occur at the shoulder in the transverse plane with the arm at $90^\circ$ abduction; this is not the movement shown by A and B.

parts.content: State two functions of cartilage.
parts.markscheme:
- State that cartilage reduces friction between bones at joints <marks>A1</marks>
- State that cartilage absorbs shock during movement <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept other valid functions such as supporting soft tissues or maintaining joint stability.</note>

parts.content: Describe the function of the frontal plane in human movement.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe that the frontal plane divides the body into anterior (front) and posterior (back) sections <marks>A1</marks>
- State that movements occurring in this plane include abduction and adduction <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify that it is important in lateral sports actions such as jumping jacks or star jumps <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe its use in analyzing side-stepping and lateral lunges <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Outline the characteristics of second-class levers with one example.
parts.markscheme:
- Outline that the load is positioned between the fulcrum and the effort <marks>A1</marks>
- State that second-class levers offer a mechanical advantage <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that they increase force output, allowing a large load to be moved with less effort <marks>A1</marks>
- Provide a correct example and identify its components (e.g., calf raise where the fulcrum is the toes, the load is the body weight, and the effort is provided by the calf muscles) <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award [1 max] for any correct example with components identified.</note>

parts.content: Identify two types of synovial joints.

<note> Any two</note>

parts.content: State the four components of a lever system.
parts.markscheme:
* lever (bone) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* fulcrum (joint) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* effort (muscle contraction) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* load / resistance (weight of body part or object) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note> Any two</note>

parts.content: State and explain five types of movement that occur at the shoulder joint.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks> 1 mark</marks> for each correct movement and explanation pair:

* Flexion: a movement that decreases the angle between two body parts <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Extension: a movement that increases the angle between two body parts <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Abduction: movement of a limb away from the midline of the body <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Adduction: movement of a limb toward the midline of the body <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Rotation: a movement where a bone or whole limb pivots or revolves around a single long axis <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept circumduction as an alternative movement: circular movement combining flexion, extension, abduction, and adduction.</note>

parts.content: Explain the functional roles of different muscle fiber types in athletic performance.
parts.markscheme:
* Type I (Slow Twitch): high aerobic capacity / endurance and low force output, suited for long-duration activities like marathons <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Type IIa (Fast Twitch Oxidative): moderate endurance and moderate force output, suited for repeated high-intensity efforts like middle-distance running <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Type IIx (Fast Twitch Glycolytic): low endurance and high force output, suited for explosive activities like weightlifting or sprinting <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Fiber composition determines training adaptations / how an athlete responds to specific training stimulus <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The balance of fiber types influences sport specificity / an athlete's predisposition for certain types of sports <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify two types of connective tissue.
parts.markscheme:
- Tendons <marks>A1</marks>
- Ligaments <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept other valid examples such as cartilage, fascia, bone, or adipose tissue.</note>

parts.content: State two functions of tendons.
parts.markscheme:
- Connect muscle to bone <marks>A1</marks>
- Transmit force to enable movement <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept other valid functions such as joint stabilization or assistance in absorbing shock.</note>

parts.content: Describe how ligaments contribute to joint stability.
parts.markscheme:
- Connect bone to bone <marks>A1</marks>
- Limit excessive or unwanted movement at the joint <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept description of providing structural support and maintaining joint alignment.</note>

parts.content: Explain the role of skeletal muscle in generating movement.
parts.markscheme:
- Muscles contract to produce force, pulling on bones to create movement at joints <marks>A1</marks>
- Muscles work in reciprocal pairs (agonists and antagonists) to allow controlled motion <marks>A1</marks>
- Muscles convert chemical energy (ATP) into mechanical energy <marks>A1</marks>
- Muscles provide stability to joints during motion and help maintain posture <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award [max 4 marks]. Accept mention of different muscle fiber types (slow-twitch and fast-twitch) and their roles in endurance versus power.</note>

parts.content: Outline the structure of a first-class lever with one sporting example.
parts.markscheme:
- A first-class lever consists of three components: a fulcrum, a load, and an effort <marks>A1</marks>
- The fulcrum is positioned between the effort and the load <marks>A1</marks>
- This arrangement can result in either mechanical advantage or disadvantage depending on the relative distances of the load and effort from the fulcrum <marks>A1</marks>
- Sporting example: Heading a football, where the neck joint acts as the fulcrum, neck muscles provide the effort, and the weight of the head acts as the load <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Accept other valid examples such as triceps extension during a throw.</note>

parts.content: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of third-class levers in sport.
parts.markscheme:
Advantages:
- Allow for a greater range of motion at the distal end of the limb <marks>A1</marks>
- Enable high speed and velocity of movement at the distal end, beneficial for throwing or kicking <marks>A1</marks>
- Well-suited for precision and coordination in sporting skills <marks>A1</marks>

Disadvantages:
- Require a greater effort force to move a relatively smaller load (mechanical disadvantage) <marks>A1</marks>
- Inefficient for lifting or moving heavy weights <marks>A1</marks>
- Can place higher strain on muscles and joints when high forces are required <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>Accept mention of agility/finesse as an advantage or increased injury risk as a disadvantage.</note>

parts.content: Describe four characteristics of skeletal muscle that contribute to movement.
parts.markscheme:
- Contractility: the ability of the muscle to generate force and shorten <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Extensibility: the ability of the muscle to stretch beyond its normal resting length <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Elasticity: the ability of the muscle to return to its original shape after being stretched <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Excitability: the ability of the muscle to respond to stimuli from the nervous system <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how joint movement in multiple planes contributes to complex sports actions such as a tennis serve.
parts.markscheme:
- Complex sports actions like a tennis serve require coordinated joint movements across all three anatomical planes: sagittal, frontal, and transverse <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The shoulder moves in the sagittal (flexion/extension), frontal (abduction), and transverse (internal/external rotation) planes to position and accelerate the arm <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The elbow undergoes flexion and extension in the sagittal plane, contributing to the preparatory phase and follow-through <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The wrist adds fine control and power through flexion/extension (sagittal) and radial/ulnar deviation (frontal) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The trunk rotates in the transverse plane and laterally flexes in the frontal plane to generate torque and maintain balance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The hips and knees extend in the sagittal plane during the jump phase, providing upward propulsion <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>(Other acceptable answers include descriptions of how coordinated use of multiple joints improves accuracy, power transfer, and injury prevention.)</note>

parts.content: Discuss how connective tissues (ligaments, tendons, fascia, and cartilage) work together to support joint stability and efficiency during explosive movement.
parts.markscheme:
- Ligaments limit excessive movement and stabilize joints <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Tendons transmit muscular force to bones for movement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fascia maintains muscle alignment and reduces friction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cartilage cushions joint surfaces and absorbs impact <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Together, these tissues reduce injury risk and increase force efficiency <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They provide mechanical support and optimize movement patterns <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
The diagram illustrates a gymnast performing a cartwheel. Which anatomical plane and axis of rotation best describe the overall body rotation during the cartwheel shown?

![Gymnast cartwheel](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-sehs-885ee940-1394-43a0-a0ba-f1ad56d2c0c9-1771953315939/1bb7713b-7a91-41a6-851b-49b78ffc7c41.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.1 Anatomical position, planes and movement  > B.1.1.2 Planes of movement and axes of rotation in human motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.1.2 Planes of movement and axes of rotation in human motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which anatomical plane and axis of rotation are primarily used in a cartwheel, directly assessing knowledge of planes of movement and axes of rotation in human motion.

options.markscheme: A
A cartwheel is characterised by lateral (sideways) rotation of the body in the frontal plane, not forward/backward rotation in the sagittal plane.
Transverse plane/longitudinal axis describes twisting (e.g., pirouette), not a cartwheel.
Movement in the frontal plane occurs about the sagittal axis, not the longitudinal axis.

options.markscheme: Incorrect: this is true for sagittal-plane movements, but it does not best explain the general plane–axis relationship across all planes.

options.content: Movements occur along the same line as the axis
options.markscheme: Incorrect: the axis is the line about which rotation occurs; movement occurs in a plane, not along the axis.

questions.specification: A diver performs a forward somersault during a competition. Which anatomical plane and axis of rotation are primarily used to execute this movement?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.1 Anatomical position, planes and movement  > B.1.1.2 Planes of movement and axes of rotation in human motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.1.2 Planes of movement and axes of rotation in human motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which anatomical plane and axis of rotation are used in a forward somersault, directly assessing knowledge of planes of movement and axes of rotation in human motion.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. Movement in the frontal plane occurs about the sagittal axis (e.g., abduction/adduction), not a forward somersault.
Incorrect. Movement in the transverse plane occurs about the vertical (longitudinal) axis (e.g., rotation), not a forward somersault.
C
Incorrect. Movement in the frontal plane occurs about the sagittal axis, not the vertical axis, and does not describe a forward somersault.

parts.content: Identify two axes of rotation used in human movement.
parts.markscheme:
- Longitudinal axis <marks>A1</marks>
- Frontal (mediolateral) axis <marks>A1</marks>
- Sagittal (anteroposterior) axis <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each axis identified, up to [2]. Accept correct spelling of "sagittal" only; do not accept "saggital".</note>

parts.content: State three effects of aging on the muscular or skeletal system that impact movement.
parts.markscheme:
- Decreased muscle mass <marks>A1</marks>
- Reduced bone density <marks>A1</marks>
- Decreased joint flexibility <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Other acceptable answers include slower muscle contraction speed and increased risk of injury. Award [1] for each effect stated, up to [3].</note>

parts.content: Describe the mechanical advantage of second-class levers and its implication in sports performance.
parts.markscheme:
- The load is positioned between the fulcrum and the effort <marks>A1</marks>
- This arrangement describes the mechanical advantage ($MA > 1$) where force output is increased with minimal effort (a small effort can move a large load) <marks>A1</marks>
- It is useful in power-based actions like jumping and pushing off the ground <marks>A1</marks>
- While it provides strong leverage, it offers a limited range of motion and speed <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Explain how differences in muscle fiber arrangement (e.g., fusiform vs. pennate) influence force and speed generation.
parts.markscheme:
- Fusiform: fibers run parallel to the long axis, allowing for a greater range of motion and contraction speed (e.g., biceps brachii) <marks>A1</marks>
- Pennate: fibers are arranged at an angle to the tendon, allowing more fibers to be packed into a given area <marks>A1</marks>
- Pennate muscles generate greater force due to higher physiological cross-sectional area <marks>A1</marks>
- Fusiform muscles contract faster but with less overall force compared to pennate muscles <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Discuss the interplay between anatomical planes and levers during multi-joint athletic movements like the clean and jerk.
parts.markscheme:
- The sagittal plane is dominant in the clean and jerk, as most joint movements (hip, knee, ankle, shoulder, and elbow flexion/extension) occur in an anterior-posterior direction <marks>A1</marks>
- The frontal plane contributes during the stabilization phase, particularly in shoulder abduction and maintaining core balance during the overhead jerk <marks>A1</marks>
- The transverse plane is engaged through rotational stability and torque required during the lift and transition phases <marks>A1</marks>
- First-class levers are utilized during head and neck stabilization where neck extensors counterbalance head tilt <marks>A1</marks>
- Second-class levers are involved at the ankle joint during plantarflexion (e.g., raising the heel to generate power during the jerk) <marks>A1</marks>
- Third-class levers dominate in elbow flexion (biceps acting between the load and fulcrum) and hip extension (gluteus maximus) to allow for speed of movement <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>Other acceptable answers include discussion of joint alignment with planes and how lever arms impact performance and injury risk.</note>

parts.markscheme:
- Surrounds/separates muscles to allow independent movement (reduces interference between adjacent muscles) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transmits force generated by muscle contraction to tendons/bone (force transmission through connective tissue) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides structural support/maintains alignment of muscle compartments <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces friction between muscles/acts as a gliding surface between tissues <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>2 marks</marks> for any two distinct correct roles.</note>

parts.content: Outline three actions of skeletal muscle relevant to producing the instep kick.
parts.markscheme:
- Produces voluntary movement by contracting and pulling on bones (e.g., knee extension via quadriceps) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maintains posture/body position against gravity (e.g., trunk/hip stabilization during the kick) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stabilizes joints/supports the skeletal structure (e.g., dynamic stabilization of the knee) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Generates heat during activity (thermogenesis during muscular work) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>3 marks</marks> for any three distinct correct actions.</note>

parts.content: With reference to the knee in Figure 1, describe two properties of synovial joints that allow movement.
parts.markscheme:
- Synovial fluid reduces friction between the articular surfaces <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Articular cartilage provides a smooth surface and absorbs shock <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Joint capsule/ligaments maintain joint integrity while allowing movement and contain synovial fluid <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>2 marks</marks> for any two distinct correct properties.</note>

parts.content: Explain how the quadriceps attachment points shown in Figure 1 influence movement mechanics at the knee joint.
parts.markscheme:
- Origin is typically the more fixed/proximal attachment and insertion is the more movable/distal attachment (during knee extension the tibia moves relative to the femur) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Relative positions of the attachment points determine the line of pull and therefore the direction of movement/torque produced <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Distance of the tendon from the joint axis (moment arm) affects torque and mechanical advantage for a given muscle force <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Greater moment arm (insertion further from the joint axis) increases torque/strength but reduces angular velocity/range for a given shortening <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Smaller moment arm (insertion closer to the joint axis) reduces torque but favours speed and range of motion <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>3 marks</marks> for any three linked correct points. Accept other correct biomechanical explanations consistent with Figure 1.</note>

parts.content: Explain how the neuromuscular system coordinates voluntary knee extension during the kick (motor cortex → motor neuron → neuromuscular junction → contraction).
parts.markscheme:
- Motor cortex initiates the signal for voluntary movement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Action potentials travel along motor neurons to the relevant motor units in the quadriceps <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At the neuromuscular junction, the nerve impulse leads to release of acetylcholine, generating an action potential in the muscle fibre <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The action potential causes calcium release in the muscle fibre, enabling actin–myosin cross-bridge cycling to produce force <marks>1 mark</marks>
- CNS integrates sensory feedback/proprioception to coordinate timing and force for smooth/precise movement <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>4 marks</marks> for any four correct, sequentially linked points showing coordination from the CNS to contraction.</note>

questions.specification: Why might anthropometric analysis be especially useful for talent identification in basketball?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.1 Anatomical position, planes and movement  > B.1.1.3 Anthropometry and its applications in sport and health science (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.1.3 Anthropometry and its applications in sport and health science (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why anthropometric analysis is useful for talent identification in basketball, focusing on body measurements/proportions like height and arm span that confer performance advantage—core application of anthropometry in sport.

options.content: It indicates an athlete’s somatotype (for example, ectomorph) as the key predictor of shooting accuracy.
It predicts coordination and agility levels.
It highlights height, arm span, and body proportions advantageous for performance.
It estimates body fat percentage as the sole determinant of on-court performance.
